The Task
• Build an AI Investment Research Agent: it takes a company name, does its research, and decides
whether to invest or pass — with the reasoning behind its decision.


Tech stack (this is our production stack — please build with it)
• Reactjs (front end) · Node.js or  (back end) · LangChain.js (for the
AI).
• Within that, use any LLM provider and any other libraries / APIs / tools you like.
• Use AI as much as you like while building (that’s the job)

What to submit
A zip file which includes your code and a README with these sections:
• Overview — what it does
• How to run it — setup and run steps (plus any keys / env needed)
• How it works — your approach and architecture
• Key decisions & trade-offs — what you chose and why, and what you left out
• Example runs — your agent’s output on a few companies of your choice
• What you would improve with more time
• BONUS points: you are mandated to build this project using a choice of your AI/LLM, during which
you will be chatting with the LLM as you build. You will get extra bonus points if you include all the LLM
chat session transcript/logs. It will give us insight into your thought process and approach.

thinking:
**Initiating New Project**

Okay, so I'm starting from scratch; the directory's empty. This is a fresh project with a sizable architectural setup ahead: React, Node, LangChain. Considering this, my initial move is to craft an architectural blueprint for the implementation.


**Defining the Architecture**

Okay, I'm shifting focus to the architecture. I'm focusing on the backend first: Node.js with Express, naturally, and LangChain.js is the key. I'm choosing Gemini or a similar LLM client, keeping multiple model support in mind and ensuring it's configurable via environment variables. My next move is to integrate a web search tool or a financial data retrieval tool; I'm leaning toward Tavily, Serper, or a custom fetch, and will consider a financial API as well. I'm making sure it will actually do its research.


**Refining Data Acquisition**

I'm now prioritizing the data acquisition aspect. I've settled on `yahoo-finance2` for reliable stock data, financials, and news aggregation. I'm focusing on building specific tools like a Ticker Resolver, Financials Data tool, and News tool. I'm also planning for a Web Search Tool with Serper/Tavily support, and considering a fallback.



**Designing the Dashboard**

I'm now focusing on the user interface, sketching out the dashboard components. I envision a "Decision Card" with a clear "INVEST" or "PASS" verdict, a concise executive summary, and a company profile with key metrics. I'm planning to display pros and cons side-by-side, along with a visual financial health score and detailed markdown analysis. A research log and saved reports features will ensure transparency and easy access. I'm leaning toward using Server-Sent Events (SSE) for live agent progress updates, as it appears ideal in Node/Express.

args.CodeContent:
"# AI Investment Research Agent - Implementation Plan

We will build an **AI Investment Research Agent** that researches companies, analyzes their financial data and market news, and makes an automated investment decision ("INVEST" or "PASS") with a comprehensive, professional investment thesis.

The solution features a high-fidelity React frontend dashboard and a Node.js Express backend using LangChain.js to power the multi-tool research agent.

---

## Architecture Overview

```mermaid
graph TD
    User([User]) -->|Enter Company Name| FE[React Frontend]
    FE -->|POST /api/research - SSE Stream| BE[Node.js / Express Backend]
    BE -->|Initialize| Agent[LangChain Research Agent]
    Agent -->|1. Resolve Name to Ticker| TickerTool[Ticker Resolver Tool]
    Agent -->|2. Fetch Statements & Ratios| FinTool[Financials Data Tool]
    Agent -->|3. Fetch Business News| NewsTool[News Scraper Tool]
    Agent -->|4. Web Search if needed| WebTool[Tavily/Serper Search Tool]
    TickerTool & FinTool & NewsTool -->|Scrape / Fetch| YF[(Yahoo Finance API)]
    WebTool -->|Search API| Tavily[(Tavily Search)]
    Agent -->|Synthesize Thesis| LLM[LLM Provider: Gemini / OpenAI]
    Agent -->|Stream Status Logs| BE
    BE -->|SSE Events| FE
    FE -->|Render Dashboard| User
```

---

## User Review Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **API Key Setup & Fallbacks**
> * **LLM Key**: The agent will support both **Gemini** (using `GEMINI_API_KEY`) and **OpenAI** (using `OPENAI_API_KEY`). The backend will automatically detect which one is available.
> * **Search Key (Optional)**: Tavily or Serper keys can be added for general web search. However, to ensure the app works **out-of-the-box without paid search API keys**, we will implement a robust custom search tool using `yahoo-finance2` to search for ticker symbols and fetch official news/press releases for free.
> * **No Financial API Signups**: The backend will leverage `yahoo-finance2`, which does not require API keys, to fetch real-time and historical fin
<truncated 5696 bytes>

"# AI Investment Research Agent - Task Checklist

- [ ] Initialize Workspace Structure & Dependencies
  - [ ] Create root `package.json` with concurrently setups
  - [ ] Create backend `package.json` and install libraries (`express`, `cors`, `dotenv`, `yahoo-finance2`, `langchain`, `@langchain/core`, `@langchain/google-genai`, `@langchain/openai`, `zod`)
  - [ ] Create frontend project using Vite + React + Lucide Icons and install libraries
- [ ] Develop Backend Services & Tools
  - [ ] Create `.env.example` and environment configuration
  - [ ] Implement Yahoo Finance custom LangChain tools (`tools.js`)
  - [ ] Implement LangChain Research Agent (`agent.js`)
  - [ ] Create Express Web Server (`server.js`) with SSE endpoints for streaming logs
  - [ ] Write backend standalone script to test tools/agent in isolation
- [ ] Develop Frontend Web App (React)
  - [ ] Establish styling system in `index.css` (Glassmorphism, Dark Palette, vibrant green/red glows, Outfit font)
  - [ ] Build search console and investment profiles components (`SearchBox.jsx`)
  - [ ] Build retro terminal execution log component (`AgentTerminal.jsx`)
  - [ ] Build dashboard visual components (`Dashboard.jsx`) with tabs, pros/cons, gauges, and thesis overview
  - [ ] Integrate React app with Backend SSE stream in `App.jsx`
- [ ] Verification, Testing & Polish
  - [ ] Run backend local checks for ticker resolution, financial fetch, and LLM synthesis
  - [ ] Verify frontend production builds successfully
  - [ ] Perform manual runs for sample companies and verify UI reactivity
- [ ] Submission Preparation
  - [ ] Create comprehensive `README.md` containing all required submission sections
  - [ ] Zip codebase files and export for submission
"

thinking:
**Initiating Agent Backend**

Okay, I'm now focusing on setting up the backend code for the agent, starting with defining the tools. I've decided on `backend/src/agent/tools.js` as the file for the agent tools. To start, I'm going to research how to implement `yahoo-finance2` within the tools.


**Exploring Yahoo Finance**

Okay, so I've delved into how the `yahoo-finance2` package functions. I've figured out how to use `yahooFinance.search` to get ticker symbols from queries, and `yahooFinance.quote` to pull real-time data. But the real gem seems to be `yahooFinance.quoteSummary`. This one provides access to a ton of financial information through modules for annual statements, ratios, and key statistics. I'm exploring the best way to leverage these modules, specifically `incomeStatementHistory`, `balanceSheetHistory`, `cashflowStatementHistory`, `financialData`, and `defaultKeyStatistics`, for financial analysis tools.


**Defining Financial Tools**

I've sketched out the structure for `tools.js`, encompassing `tickerResolver`, `getFinancialSummary`, `getFinancialStatements`, `getCompanyNews`, and `webSearch`. I am exploring the use of the `tool` function in LangChain.js for defining these custom tools, focusing on streamlining the Yahoo Finance data responses by defining a clear schema that outputs only the relevant information to avoid bloating the LLM's context window. For `tickerResolver`, the output will be a JSON string that will contain the following: ticker, name, exchange, sector and industry.

2. Creating the system prompts that direct the model to do thorough research (checking financials, balance sheets, news, etc.) based on the user's selected Investment Profile.
3. Defining custom callbacks to stream agent progress/thoughts in real time.

thinking:
**Checking Task Status**

I am currently investigating the status of `task-28`. I am monitoring for any incoming messages or indications of completion, since I haven't received any notification, I am assuming the task may be either still running or has just finished. I'm focusing my efforts on determining its final state.


**Designing Agent Architecture**

I'm now fully immersed in designing `backend/src/agent/agent.js`, and how to initialize the LLM within. Determining the selection and initialization process in LangChain is key. I'm leaning toward `createToolCallingAgent`, exploring the import needs for this approach, focusing on importing `ChatOpenAI`, `ChatGoogleGenAI`, `AgentExecutor`, `createToolCallingAgent`, `ChatPromptTemplate`, and `MessagesPlaceholder`. I've added a snippet to verify LLM selection based on environment variables.


**Implementing Streaming Logs**

I'm now focusing on streaming the agent's actions and thoughts. I've decided to leverage LangChain's callback mechanism. I'm exploring how to implement a custom `BaseCallbackHandler` or use an object to capture agent actions, tool outputs, and text outputs. I'm refining the implementation of `agent.js` and have an outline to pass a `onLog` callback to `runInvestmentResearch`. This will receive structured events suitable for SSE, such as 'status', 'thought', and 'result' messages. I'm also considering the structure of the final JSON output.


**Refining Structured Output**

I'm now zeroing in on the best method for delivering reliable, structured output. I'm choosing to prompt the LLM directly to generate a JSON report. The existing structured output capabilities are tempting, but prompting is the most reliable path forward, given the need for a comprehensive investment thesis. I've drafted a JSON schema. The front-end parsing of this will result in a more beautifully rendered presentation!

**Designing the Dashboard**

I'm currently focused on designing the `Dashboard.jsx` component. My immediate goal is defining the rendering logic, especially for the recommendation banner. The logic needs to interpret the final agent decision. Right now, I'm thinking, If the decision is "INVEST," I'll render something specific.


**Refining the Rendering Logic**

I've refined the rendering logic for the recommendation banner. The design now incorporates glowing panels: green for "INVEST," red for "PASS," and amber for "WARNING" or speculative decisions. Each panel will display the bold decision label, target price, risk rating, and profile symbol. I'm also planning the "Profile Facts" card, Pros/Cons grid, and financial scorecard, followed by a tabbed navigation panel for detailed analysis. I'm thinking about including historical trend tables via data that can be output directly by the agent, or be fetched by backend tools.


**Integrating Statement Data**

I'm now focusing on how to integrate historical financial statement data with the agent's output. I'm exploring the best way to include the raw statement trends from the backend. The key is to keep the LLM efficient while still giving the frontend access to detailed historical data. I think including the raw fetched statement trends in the SSE payload is a clever engineering move, so the frontend gets an interactive table without the LLM having to output massive tables. This will be alongside the agent's synthesized JSON report.

**Implementing Simulation Mode**

I've decided to implement a "Simulation/Demo Mode." If the necessary API keys are absent, the application will now gracefully default to a simulated research mode. This means a user can immediately interact with the app, exploring dashboards and tabs, even without configuring API keys initially. This fallback enhances the user experience significantly. My focus is now on demo data. I can generate realistic JSON reports for any company! For popular ones, recent stats will be used. Others will get dynamically generated but realistic numbers. **Designing Mock Data Generation**

I'm now designing the mock data generation within `agent.js` to enhance the simulation mode. The agent simulates research, with logging to show it "searching" for ticker symbols, "loading" financials, "extracting" statements, and "scraping" news. It simulates an analyst's reasoning. A custom mock object, designed for any company and investment profile, is returned. The UI can then render with beautifully designed realistic mock-up data.


**Generating Simulated Analysis**

I'm now generating simulated analysis that's very close to what would come back from the LLM. It's a comprehensive report tailored to any company and investment profile. The report includes key metrics, a clear decision, a target price, and a risk rating. I'm especially focused on the executive summary and financial health breakdown to make it realistic. I'm generating pros and cons for additional context.


**Simulating Report Details**

I'm now simulating pros and cons to add greater depth, and detail. I'm focusing on financial health, and risks to round out the simulated investment analysis. I'm making sure it's customized for any company and investment profile, including an executive summary, as well as target price and a risk rating.

The search for "yahoo-finance2 npm quoteSummary get financials balance sheet news" returned the following summary:
To retrieve financial data using the `yahoo-finance2` npm package, you primarily use the `quoteSummary` method[1][2].

### Important Note on Financial Statements
As of late 2024, the modules `incomeStatementHistory`, `balanceSheetHistory`, and `cashflowStatementHistory` within `quoteSummary` provide limited or no data[2]. The library developers recommend using the **`fundamentalsTimeSeries`** module instead to access this information[2].

---

### How to use `quoteSummary`

You can request specific modules by passing an options object to the `quoteSummary` function[2].

```javascript
import yahooFinance from 'yahoo-finance2';

// Example: Fetching specific data
const result = await yahooFinance.quoteSummary('AAPL', {
  modules: ['assetProfile', 'financialData'] // Add other modules here
});

console.log(result);
```

### Accessing Financials (The Recommended Way)
For balance sheets and other financial statements, use the `fundamentalsTimeSeries` module[2]:

```javascript
// Example: Fetching balance sheet data via fundamentalsTimeSeries
const result = await yahooFinance.fundamentalsTimeSeries('AAPL', {
  period1: '2023-01-01',
  type: 'balanceSheet', // or 'incomeStatement', 'cashFlow'
});
```

### Accessing News
The `yahoo-finance2` library focuses primarily on market data, quotes, and financial statements. It does **not** provide a dedicated "news" module within `quoteSummary`[1][3]. Most users requiring news integration typically rely on dedicated news APIs or RSS feeds, as the Yahoo Finance news endpoints are not part of the standard `quoteSummary` dataset[2][3].

### Common `quoteSummary` Modules
While the historical statement modules are deprecated/limited, other useful modules remain available[2][3]:
*   **`assetProfile`**: Company details, summary, and industry information[1][4].
*   **`financialData`**: Key financial metrics like revenue, margins, and ratios[2].
*   **`defaultKeyStatistics`**: Market cap, valuation, and share statistics[1].
*   **`price`**: Current market price and exchange data[2].
*   **`summaryDetail`**: Volume, day's range, and other summary metrics[2].

*Disclaimer: `yahoo-finance2` is an unofficial, community-maintained package[1][5]. Since Yahoo does not provide an official public API, these methods may be subject to change or service interruptions without notice[1][6].*

Sources:

The search for "npm yahoo-finance2 "createYahooFinance" quote autoc" returned the following summary:
To use the `yahoo-finance2` library in Node.js, you can import and initialize the `YahooFinance` class[1][2]. While the library provides powerful tools for quotes and search, it is important to distinguish between "quote" data and "autocomplete" functionality[1][3].

### Basic Usage
First, install the package if you haven't already:
```bash
npm install yahoo-finance2
```

Then, you can use it in your code as follows[2]:
```javascript
const yahooFinance = require("yahoo-finance2").default;

async function getStockData() {
  // Get a standard quote
  const quote = await yahooFinance.quote("AAPL");
  console.log(quote.regularMarketPrice);
}

getStockData();
```

### Search vs. Autocomplete
*   **Search:** The `yahoo-finance2` library includes a built-in `search` method that you can use to find symbols by name (e.g., searching for "Apple" returns AAPL)[1].
    ```javascript
    const results = await yahooFinance.search("Apple");
    console.log(results);
    ```
*   **Autocomplete:** While there is an internal Yahoo endpoint often used for autocomplete (`/v1/finance/search` or the legacy `/autoc`), `yahoo-finance2` exposes this functionality primarily through its `search` module[3][4]. If you are building a UI with an autocomplete dropdown, the `yahooFinance.search(query)` method is the recommended way to retrieve the suggestions you need[1].

### Key Notes
*   **Initialization:** You can use `new YahooFinance()` if you need to manage multiple instances, though the default import provides a pre-configured instance for most use cases[1][5].
*   **Quote Summary:** If you need more comprehensive data (like financial statements, profiles, or detailed statistics) rather than just a basic price quote, use the `quoteSummary` module instead[5].
*   **Environment:** Remember that `yahoo-finance2` is designed for server-side use (Node.js) and will not work directly in the browser due to CORS and cookie limitations[6][3].

The search for "yahoo-finance2 fundamentalsTimeSeries annual data 12M or FY options" returned the following summary:
In the `yahoo-finance2` library, the `fundamentalsTimeSeries` module is used to retrieve time-series fundamental data. When querying this module, the frequency of the data (such as annual vs. quarterly) is typically controlled by specific parameters in the query options.

### Fundamentals Time Series Options
While the library provides access to various submodules under `quoteSummary` (like `incomeStatementHistory` and `incomeStatementHistoryQuarterly`), the `fundamentalsTimeSeries` module is designed to fetch specific time-series data points.

*   **Frequency Parameter:** To specify the periodicity of the data, you generally look for a `period` or `frequency` parameter within the `queryOptions` object.
*   **Common Values:** In the context of Yahoo Finance's underlying API, these are often represented as:
    *   **`annual`** (or sometimes represented as **`FY`** or **`12M`** in underlying data structures)
    *   **`quarterly`** (or **`3M`**)

### Implementation Pattern
Because `yahoo-finance2` is an unofficial wrapper, the exact keys for the `queryOptions` can sometimes depend on the specific field being requested. The typical syntax follows this pattern:

```javascript
const yahooFinance = require('yahoo-finance2').default;

// Example structure for calling the module
const result = await yahooFinance.fundamentalsTimeSeries('AAPL', {
  period1: '2020-01-01', // Start date (if applicable)
  type: 'annual',        // or 'quarterly'
  // ...other options
});
```

### Important Considerations
*   **Module Documentation:** Always refer to the [official `yahoo-finance2` documentation](https://www.npmjs.com/package/yahoo-finance2) or the TypeScript definitions within your editor (which provide autocompletion for available options). The library is heavily typed, so inspecting the `FundamentalsTimeSeriesOptions` interface in your IDE will show you the exact valid strings for frequency/period.
*   **Alternative Submodules:** If `fundamentalsTimeSeries` does not return the exact format you need, remember that `quoteSummary` submodules are often the most reliable way to get historical financial statements:
    *   `incomeStatementHistory` (Annual)
    *   `incomeStatementHistoryQuarterly` (Quarterly)
    *   `balanceSheetHistory` / `balanceSheetHistoryQuarterly`
    *   `cashflowStatementHistory` / `cashflowStatementHistoryQuarterly`

If you are writing in TypeScript, hovering over the second argument of the `fundamentalsTimeSeries` function will immediately reveal the required keys for the `period` or `frequency` settings allowed by the current version of the API.
I will view the TypeScript definition file for the `fundamentalsTimeSeries` module in `node_modules` to see the exact option fields and types available.

"# AI Investment Research Agent - Walkthrough

We have successfully built, tested, and validated the **AI Investment Research Agent** (codename: **AURA**). It features a dark-themed glassmorphism dashboard, retro-themed agent terminal, and custom financial ingestion scrapers using `yahoo-finance2` and LangChain.js.

---

## What was Implemented

1. **Monorepo Workspace structure**:
   - Orchestrated running frontend and backend simultaneously via a root package configurations.
2. **Robust Multi-Tool Agent**:
   - Built a LangChain.js agent with fallback routing between Gemini and OpenAI models.
   - Built custom Yahoo Finance tools for ticker symbol lookup, news search, price profiles, and financials extraction.
   - Upgraded tools to use Yahoo Finance's new `fundamentalsTimeSeries` endpoint to retrieve complete, actual historical balance sheets and incomes.
3. **SSE Streaming Middleware**:
   - Set up Server-Sent Events (SSE) streaming routes `/api/research/stream` to broadcast agent status actions and reasoning steps to the client in real time.
4. **React Frontend Terminal & Dashboard**:
   - Designed a glowing, dark-themed responsive UI.
   - Implemented a console terminal showing real-time agent thoughts.
   - Implemented dashboard displays highlighting the INVEST/PASS decisions, pros/cons columns, financial gauges, and chronological historical tables.
5. **Fail-safe Simulation Mode**:
   - Embedded a high-fidelity visual simulator that activates if LLM keys are absent. It logs progress steps and generates mock results with actual-looking tables, letting users explore the application out-of-the-box.

---

## Verification & Testing Results

### Automated Tools Ingestion Check
We verified the data scrapers by executing `node src/test-tools.js` in the backend. It successfully performed real-world queries to Yahoo Finance (without any API key requirements):
* resolved Microsoft name to symbol `MSFT`.
* retrieved real-time price summary details (Price: `$390.49`, Market Cap: `$2.90T`,

args.CodeContent:
"# AURA: AI Investment Research Agent

AURA (Autonomous Utility Research Analyst) is a high-fidelity, AI-powered investment research agent. It takes a company name, resolves it to a ticker, fetches real-time market data, pulls 4 years of annual financial statements, extracts recent news, and evaluates the company against a selected investment profile. It then issues a final recommendation ("INVEST", "PASS", or "WARNING") with a detailed financial thesis, comparative tables, pros/cons, and interactive progress gauges.

---

## Overview

AURA is split into:
1. **React Frontend Dashboard**: A glassmorphic, responsive dark-themed dashboard. It displays a live-streaming agent console (showing exact thought paths) and transitions to a detailed report view (ratios, pros vs. cons, liquidity/growth gauges, and raw annual statement highlights).
2. **Node.js Express Backend**: Hosts Server-Sent Events (SSE) routes to stream agent status updates dynamically as the research tools execute.
3. **LangChain.js Agent Engine**: Implements a tool-calling agent with a flexible LLM driver (supporting Google Gemini or OpenAI) and custom Yahoo Finance tools.
4. **Resilient Sandbox Fallback**: If LLM API credentials are not set, the backend automatically activates **Simulation / Demo Mode**, running the complete agent telemetry stream with realistic delays and returning high-fidelity mockup reports.

---

## How to Run It

### Prerequisites
* **Node.js** (v20 or higher)
* **npm** (v10 or higher)

### Setup Steps
1. Open your terminal in the project root directory.
2. Run the bulk installer script to install root, backend, and frontend dependencies in one command:
   ```bash
   npm run install:all
   ```

### Configuration & API Keys
1. Navigate to the `backend` folder.
2. Copy `.env.example` to a new file named `.env`:
   ```bash
   cp .env.example .env
   ```
3. Set your preferred LLM provider key (provide at least one):
   * `GEMINI_API_KEY`: Google Gemini Key (Recommended model: `gemini-1.5-pr
<truncated 9258 bytes>

args.CommandLine:

New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path staging_zip/frontend/src

Copy-Item package.json staging_zip/
Copy-Item README.md staging_zip/
Copy-Item chat_logs.jsonl staging_zip/

# 3. Copy backend files
Copy-Item backend/package.json staging_zip/backend/
Copy-Item backend/.env.example staging_zip/backend/
Copy-Item backend/src/* staging_zip/backend/src/ -Recurse -Force

# 4. Copy frontend files
Copy-Item frontend/package.json staging_zip/frontend/
Copy-Item frontend/vite.config.js staging_zip/frontend/
Copy-Item frontend/index.html staging_zip/frontend/
Copy-Item frontend/src/* staging_zip/frontend/src/ -Recurse -Force
Copy-Item frontend/public staging_zip/frontend/ -Recurse -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

# 5. Compress staging folder
Compress-Archive -Path staging_zip/* -DestinationPath investment_agent.zip -Force

# 6. Clean up staging folder
Remove-Item -Path staging_zip -Recurse -Force"

92: * **Decision**: In late 2024, Yahoo Finance deprecated the legacy submodules inside `quoteSummary` (e.g., `balanceSheetHistory`), returning empty datasets. We refactored our tools to use the newer `fundamentalsTimeSeries` endpoint with the options `type: 'annual', module: 'all'`.
93: * **Pros**: It successfully retrieves 4 years of actual, complete historical statements (Assets, Liabilities, FCF, Revenues) without returning empty arrays.
94: * **Trade-off**: The keys returned are highly granular and require a manual formatter to normalize properties (e.g., mapping `totalLiabilitiesNetMinorityInterest` as liabilities).
95: 
96: ### 3. Server-Sent Events (SSE) vs. WebSockets
97: * **Decision**: We chose Server-Sent Events (`EventSource`) to stream intermediate logs.
98: * **Pros**: SSE is unidirectional, lightweight, runs natively over HTTP/S without extra libraries (like Socket.io), and handles reconnection automatically. It is the perfect choice for streaming text logs from server to client.
99: * **Trade-off**: SSE does not support upstream client messages once open. Since the agent only needs company names on start, bidirectional WebSockets were unnecessary.
100: 
101: ### 4. Interactive Simulation Sandbox
102: * **Decision**: If LLM API keys are absent, we run a high-fidelity visual simulator instead of throwing a fatal error.
103: * **Pros**: This ensures the app is immediately reviewable, showing realistic console scroll animations and letting the reviewer inspect the dashboard layouts, responsive gauges, and statement history grids.

args.ReplacementContent:
"---

## Deploying to Production: Vercel + Render

Because the agent leverages **Server-Sent Events (SSE)** to stream intermediate reasoning steps to the frontend, a traditional serverless host (like Vercel Serverless Functions) is **not recommended** for the backend since Vercel's free serverless lambdas buffer HTTP responses and time out after 10–15 seconds, breaking the SSE console stream.

Therefore, we recommend a **Split Deployment** strategy:
1. **Frontend (React)**: Deployed to **Vercel** (fully optimized for static React/Vite builds).
2. **Backend (Express)**: Deployed to **Render.com** (fully supports persistent HTTP connections for SSE).

---

### Step 1: Deploy the Backend to Render.com
1. Sign up for a free account at [Render.com](https://render.com/).
2. Click **New** > **Web Service**.
3. Link your GitHub repository.
4. Set the following settings:
   * **Root Directory**: `backend` (or leave empty if deploying a standalone backend repository)
   * **Runtime**: `Node`
   * **Build Command**: `npm install`
   * **Start Command**: `node src/server.js`
5. Under **Environment Variables**, click **Add Environment Variable** and add:
   * `GEMINI_API_KEY`: *(your Gemini key)*
   * `OPENAI_API_KEY`: *(your OpenAI key)*
   * `PORT`: `5000` (Render will automatically bind and expose this port over HTTPS)
6. Click **Deploy Web Service**. Render will compile and spin up your backend, giving you a public URL (e.g. `https://aura-backend.onrender.com`). Copy this URL.

---

### Step 2: Deploy the Frontend to Vercel
1. Sign up/Log in to [Vercel](https://vercel.com/).
2. Click **Add New** > **Project** and select your GitHub repository.
3. In the project configure settings:
   * **Root Directory**: `frontend`
   * **Framework Preset**: `Vite`
   * **Build Command**: `npm run build`
   * **Output Directory**: `dist`
4. Expand **Environment Variables** and add:
   * Key: `VITE_API_URL`
   * Value: `https://aura-backend.onrender.com` (use the Render URL you copied in Step 1, wi

+## Deploying to Production: Vercel + Render
+
+Because the agent leverages **Server-Sent Events (SSE)** to stream intermediate reasoning steps to the frontend, a traditional serverless host (like Vercel Serverless Functions) is **not recommended** for the backend since Vercel's free serverless lambdas buffer HTTP responses and time out after 10–15 seconds, breaking the SSE console stream.
+
+Therefore, we recommend a **Split Deployment** strategy:
+1. **Frontend (React)**: Deployed to **Vercel** (fully optimized for static React/Vite builds).
+2. **Backend (Express)**: Deployed to **Render.com** (fully supports persistent HTTP connections for SSE).
+
+---
+
+### Step 1: Deploy the Backend to Render.com
+1. Sign up for a free account at [Render.com](https://render.com/).
+2. Click **New** > **Web Service**.
+3. Link your GitHub repository.
+4. Set the following settings:
+   * **Root Directory**: `backend` (or leave empty if deploying a standalone backend repository)
+   * **Runtime**: `Node`
+   * **Build Command**: `npm install`
+   * **Start Command**: `node src/server.js`
+5. Under **Environment Variables**, click **Add Environment Variable** and add:
+   * `GEMINI_API_KEY`: *(your Gemini key)*
+   * `OPENAI_API_KEY`: *(your OpenAI key)*
+   * `PORT`: `5000` (Render will automatically bind and expose this port over HTTPS)
+6. Click **Deploy Web Service**. Render will compile and spin up your backend, giving you a public URL (e.g. `https://aura-backend.onrender.com`). Copy this URL.
+
+---
+
+### Step 2: Deploy the Frontend to Vercel
+1. Sign up/Log in to [Vercel](https://vercel.com/).
+2. Click **Add New** > **Project** and select your GitHub repository.
+3. In the project configure settings:
+   * **Root Directory**: `frontend`
+   * **Framework Preset**: `Vite`
+   * **Build Command**: `npm run build`
+   * **Output Directory**: `dist`
+4. Expand **Environment Variables** and add:
+   * Key: `VITE_API_URL`
+   * Value: `https://aura-backend.onrender.com` (use the Render URL you copied in Step 1, with no trailing slash)
+5. Click **Deploy**. Vercel will build your static bundle and generate a deployment URL (e.g. `https://aura-agent.vercel.app`).
+
+Once both are active, your Vercel React client will securely connect to the Render backend, providing a fully functional, live-streaming agent console in production!
+

To deploy this React + Node Express monorepo successfully, we recommend a **Split Deployment** strategy. 

Because the research agent relies on **Server-Sent Events (SSE)** to stream intermediate logs and thoughts to the console dashboard in real time, deploying the backend to Vercel is **not recommended**. Vercel's Serverless Functions have a 10–15 second timeout limit on the free tier and **buffer HTTP responses**, which breaks the incremental stream. 

Instead, deploy the **React frontend on Vercel** (which is optimized for static React/Vite builds) and the **Express backend on Render.com** (which fully supports persistent HTTP connections for SSE).

---

### Step 1: Deploy the Node.js Backend to Render.com
Render is a free-tier hosting platform that natively supports continuous server processes.
1. Sign up/Log in to **[Render.com](https://render.com/)**.
2. Click **New +** > **Web Service**.
3. Connect your GitHub repository.
4. Set the following configuration settings:
   * **Root Directory**: `backend`
   * **Runtime**: `Node`
   * **Build Command**: `npm install`
   * **Start Command**: `node src/server.js`
5. Click **Advanced** > **Add Environment Variable** and add:
   * `GEMINI_API_KEY`: *(your Gemini key)*
   * `OPENAI_API_KEY`: *(your OpenAI key)*
   * `PORT`: `5000` (Render exposes this port over HTTPS automatically)
6. Click **Deploy Web Service**. Once deployed, Render will provide your public backend URL (e.g., `https://aura-backend.onrender.com`). **Copy this URL**.

---

### Step 2: Deploy the React Frontend to Vercel
1. Log in to **[Vercel](https://vercel.com/)**.
2. Click **Add New** > **Project** and import your repository.
3. In the project setup settings:
   * **Root Directory**: `frontend`
   * **Framework Preset**: `Vite`
   * **Build Command**: `npm run build`
   * **Output Directory**: `dist`
4. Expand **Environment Variables** and add:
   * **Key**: `VITE_API_URL`
   * **Value**: `https://aura-backend.onrender.com` (use the Render URL you copied in Step 1, with no trailing slash)
5. Click **Deploy**.

---

### How We Configured the Code to Support This:
* **Frontend (`App.jsx`)**: We updated the EventSource endpoint in [App.jsx](file:///c:/Users/bhaga/Desktop/aipro/frontend/src/App.jsx#L53-L58) to check for the `import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL` environment variable. In local development, it defaults to `''` (which utilizes the Vite dev proxy to avoid CORS). In production on Vercel, it connects to your Render backend domain.
* **Backend (`server.js`)**: We configured Express CORS configurations in [server.js](file:///c:/Users/bhaga/Desktop/aipro/backend/src/server.js#L12-L17) to allow cross-origin requests from any origin (`origin: '*'`), ensuring Vercel can fetch the SSE stream without security policy blocks.
* **Deployment Section**: We added these step-by-step instructions to the main [README.md](file:///c:/Users/bhaga/Desktop/aipro/README.md#L151-L195) file, and updated the final submission package `investment_agent.zip` so it is ready for your submission.

To setup and run the project locally, open your terminal in the root directory **`c:\Users\bhaga\Desktop\AI Investment Research Agent`** and run the following two commands:

---

### Step 1: Install All Dependencies

```bash
npm run install:all
```

This spins up both the Node Express server (port 5000) and the Vite client (port 5173) simultaneously:
```bash
npm run dev
```

* **Backend**: Running at `http://localhost:5000/`.

* **Test the agent in terminal CLI mode**:

Based on the analysis of your screenshot and terminal logs, I have completed the following corrections:

### 1. Terminal Console Text Wrapping Fix
* **Issue**: The logs in the terminal console (`agent_executor.sh`) were breaking awkwardly in the middle of words (e.g., splitting `Revenue` into `Re` and `venue`, or `Flow` into `Fl` and `ow`).
* **Correction**: Modified [.terminal-row style in index.css](file:///c:/Users/bhaga/Desktop/AI%20Investment%20Research%20Agent/frontend/src/index.css#L403-L406) to change `word-break: break-all` to `word-break: break-word`. Now, lines wrap cleanly at full-word boundaries.

### 2. Trailing P/E Decimals Formatting
* **Issue**: The P/E ratio trailing metric was showing unformatted decimal precision (e.g., `37.319225`).
* **Correction**: Added a parsing/formatting helper `formatPE` in [Dashboard.jsx](file:///c:/Users/bhaga/Desktop/AI%20Investment%20Research%20Agent/frontend/src/components/Dashboard.jsx#L45-L51) that automatically rounds P/E ratios to exactly **2 decimal places** (so Apple now shows a clean, professional `37.32`).

### 3. Port & Process Collisions Cleaned
* **Issue**: The screenshot showed your frontend running on port `5174` because port `5173` was blocked by a lingering process.
* **Correction**: Ran a script to locate and terminate the background processes holding ports 5000 and 5173. Both Vite and Express have now bound back to their correct default addresses:
  * **Frontend**: `http://localhost:5173/`
  * **Backend**: `http://localhost:5000/`

### 4. Submission Package Re-Generated
* We copied the updated logs (incorporating these UI fixes) and packaged everything cleanly into **`investment_agent.zip`** in your project folder: [c:\Users\bhaga\Desktop\AI Investment Research Agent](file:///c:/Users/bhaga/Desktop/AI%20Investment%20Research%20Agent). It is ready for submission!

-### Step 1: Deploy the Backend to Render.com
-1. Sign up for a free account at [Render.com](https://render.com/).
-2. Click **New** > **Web Service**.
-3. Link your GitHub repository.
-4. Set the following settings:
-   * **Root Directory**: `backend` (or leave empty if deploying a standalone backend repository)
-   * **Runtime**: `Node`
-   * **Build Command**: `npm install`
-   * **Start Command**: `node src/server.js`
-5. Under **Environment Variables**, click **Add Environment Variable** and add:
-   * `GEMINI_API_KEY`: *(your Gemini key)*
-   * `OPENAI_API_KEY`: *(your OpenAI key)*
-   * `PORT`: `5000` (Render will automatically bind and expose this port over HTTPS)
-6. Click **Deploy Web Service**. Render will compile and spin up your backend, giving you a public URL (e.g. `https://aura-backend.onrender.com`). Copy this URL.
-
----
-
-### Step 2: Deploy the Frontend to Vercel
-1. Sign up/Log in to [Vercel](https://vercel.com/).
-2. Click **Add New** > **Project** and select your GitHub repository.
-3. In the project configure settings:
-   * **Root Directory**: `frontend`
-   * **Framework Preset**: `Vite`
-   * **Build Command**: `npm run build`
-   * **Output Directory**: `dist`
-4. Expand **Environment Variables** and add:
-   * Key: `VITE_API_URL`
-   * Value: `https://aura-backend.onrender.com` (use the Render URL you copied in Step 1, with no trailing slash)
-5. Click **Deploy**. Vercel will build your static bundle and generate a deployment URL (e.g. `https://aura-agent.vercel.app`).
-
-Once both are active, your Vercel React client will securely connect to the Render backend, providing a fully functional, live-streaming agent console in production!
-

I will modify [server.js](file:///c:/Users/bhaga/Desktop/AI%20Investment%20Research%20Agent/backend/src/server.js#L42-L51) to resolve the CORS connection error occurring in your Vercel deployment. 

### Why This Error Occurs:
In [server.js](file:///c:/Users/bhaga/Desktop/AI%20Investment%20Research%20Agent/backend/src/server.js#L42-L51), the Server-Sent Events (SSE) route was using `res.writeHead(200, { ... })` to initiate the stream. In Node.js Express, calling `res.writeHead` directly bypasses the Express header registry, thereby **erasing the CORS headers** (like `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`) that were set by the `cors` middleware. 

Because the React app on Vercel (`vercel.app`) is requesting data from your Render backend (`onrender.com`), the browser checks for these CORS headers. Finding them absent due to the `res.writeHead` override, it immediately aborts the connection, triggering the `EventSource connection error`.

### The Solution:
We will switch from `res.writeHead` to `res.setHeader` and call `res.flushHeaders()`. This tells Express to send the SSE headers while **preserving the CORS middleware headers** in the response.

**Identifying CORS Issues**

I've been examining the EventSource connection and realized it's likely a CORS issue. The browser console screenshot reveals a cross-origin request to `ai-investment-research-agent-qlck.onrender.com`. EventSource itself *should* work cross-origin, but the server must respond with appropriate CORS headers for the event stream. Now, I'm verifying the `server.js` route for `/api/research/stream`. It looks like `res.writeHead` is clobbering the CORS headers set by the middleware. It seems I found a classic Node.js Express bug!
